turns.content: I'm preparing for my first Rust interview at a game studio, and I keep getting confused about how Bevy schedules work. My understanding is that if I put two systems in the same SystemSet, they just run together without any specific order, right?

That's actually a common misconception. Systems within a single SystemSet are not guaranteed to run in any particular order unless you explicitly define it using the before or after methods on the SystemSet itself. The framework will execute all systems in that set sequentially, but you lose control over which one runs first if they interact with shared data like components or entities.

So does that mean I should just put my heavy physics system and my rendering update in different sets to avoid conflicts? But what about those state-driven schedules? How do you actually use the condition logic?

Exactly, separating them is a good start for avoiding race conditions. For state-driven scheduling, you can attach custom components like a ScheduleCondition to entities and then query those in your SystemSet using the run_conditions filter. Think of it like this: imagine a factory assembly line where certain workers only show up if specific parts are present on the conveyor belt; Bevy's schedules let you control exactly which 'workers' (systems) get activated based on what state the world is in.

I'm getting a panic when trying to add an entity to a system set in my Bevy project. The error message says the system is not compatible with the current schedule configuration, even though I think I've added all necessary traits correctly.

It sounds like you might be missing an explicit before or after relationship between your system and another running system in that same schedule. In Bevy, when using SystemSet, the ordering is crucial; if two systems run on the same set without a defined order, they can conflict. You should check if your system needs to run before or after something else using the before/after methods on the SystemSet. Also, ensure you aren't relying on automatic scheduling for complex logic, as Bevy's default behavior might not handle your specific requirements correctly.
Wait, so I shouldn't just use a single SystemSet? Can I have multiple sets running in parallel?

You can absolutely run multiple system sets in parallel if they don't conflict. However, the key is to define clear ordering between them using before/after relationships when you do combine systems into one schedule. Bevy's Schedule builder allows this flexibility, but remember that without explicit dependencies, the runtime might choose an arbitrary order which could break your logic if it assumes a specific sequence. It's often safer to keep independent sets separate unless they truly need to interact.

Hey, I've been reading about Bevy's system scheduling but I'm still confused about how the ordering works when using SystemSet. I read that you can define a before or after relationship between sets, but I don't fully understand what happens if two systems try to modify different components in the same component type without explicit ordering.

That's a common point of confusion. Think of it like this: imagine you have two chefs, one who puts ingredients on the table (System A) and another who cooks them (System B). If System A says 'put food' and System B says 'cook food', but neither knows about the other, they might both try to touch the same tray at once causing chaos. In Bevy, this translates to systems in the same schedule potentially seeing different versions of components depending on when their system runs relative to others.
So if I have two systems modifying the same component type without ordering, they might see inconsistent states?

Exactly. Bevy guarantees that a system only sees components in their current state unless explicitly told otherwise through the system's run conditions or scheduling logic. If you want System A to modify Component X and then System B to use that modified value, you need to ensure they're in different schedules with proper ordering so System A runs before System B.
But what if I just add a RunOnce condition? Does that solve the problem?

RunOnce prevents a system from running multiple times within the same frame, but it doesn't control the ordering between different systems. You still need to explicitly define which schedule comes before or after using SystemSet::before() or SystemSet::after(). Think of RunOnce like saying 'only run once per frame' while the ordering is about 'which frame's data does this system see'.
Got it, so I need to explicitly order my schedules if they interact. One more thing though - what happens with state-driven schedules?

State-driven schedules allow a system to run only when certain conditions are met, like checking if a component exists or has specific data. This is useful for optimizing performance but remember that even with these optimizations, the fundamental ordering rules still apply - you can't rely on state checks alone to determine execution order between conflicting systems.

I'm trying to build a game where the player can switch between running on a treadmill or swimming in a pool, but I'm confused about how Bevy handles these two different schedules. If I just put both systems into a single SystemSet, won't they always run at once regardless of which mode is active?

You've hit the core distinction between scheduling and execution order. A single SystemSet runs all its systems in every frame unless you use state-driven schedules with a component like ComponentState. To make them exclusive, you need two separate SystemSets named treadmill_system_set and pool_system_set, then attach only one of those sets to your entity based on the current game mode.
Okay so I create two different SystemSet instances? But what if I want a system that runs after treadmill but before pool in some cases, like applying drag force only when moving?

You can't mix them into one set for ordering unless you use the before and after keywords within a single SystemSet definition. For true conditional execution based on state, stick to two separate sets and manage their attachment with ComponentState or a simple boolean flag component. This avoids needing complex dependency graphs just to control when systems fire.

I'm new to Rust and Bevy, so I thought a Schedule would just be a simple list of system sets running in order. Can I define my main game loop by creating two SystemSet instances and putting them into a single Schedule with an 'after' relationship? My CI is failing because the build script can't find the correct trait implementations for these schedules.

You are misunderstanding how Schedules work; they do not just hold SystemSets in a list. A Schedule requires an Entity, specifically a Plugin, to be attached to it via impls of the Plugin trait. The Scheduler will only pick up systems that match those Plugin traits. To fix your CI failure, you must ensure every system set is properly registered with its corresponding Plugin and that the schedule itself is correctly configured as a plugin. You also need to verify that all the required types like SystemSet are imported from bevy::prelude or bevy::schedule.
